day1.title: Intro & Setup: Core Fundamentals
day1.definition: Setting up the environment (browser console and Node.js), writing the first script, understanding where JavaScript executes.
day1.details: We cover how JavaScript is executed in the **Browser** vs. **Node.js**. Learn to use **`console.log()`** for output and comments. Understand the use of the `<script>` tag in HTML and the concept of **Strict Mode** (`'use strict'`). The day concludes with writing and executing a simple 'Hello World' script.
day1.extra: Key concepts: Browser vs. Node.js, `console.log()`, Script tag placement, Strict Mode, Semicolons

day2.title: Variables & Core Data Types
day2.definition: Mastering variable declaration (**`let`, `const`, `var`**) and primary data types: **`string`, `number`, `boolean`, `null`, `undefined`**.
day2.details: Focus on the critical difference between **`let`** (mutable, block-scoped) and **`const`** (immutable, block-scoped), and why **`var`** is generally avoided due to its function scope and hoisting issues. Inspect types using the **`typeof`** operator. Explore **Type Coercion** and the difference between loose (`==`) and **strict equality (`===`)**, recommending the latter.
day2.code:
let count = 42; // Number
const userName = 'Ali'; // String
let isLogged = true; // Boolean

console.log(typeof count, typeof userName);
console.log(5 == '5'); // True (coercion)
console.log(5 === '5'); // False (strict)
day2.extra: `let` vs `const` vs `var`, Strict vs. Loose Equality, Type Coercion, Primitive Data Types, `typeof` operator

day3.title: Operators & Control Flow: Decisions
day3.definition: Using **Arithmetic**, **Comparison**, and **Logical Operators** to form complex expressions, and controlling execution with **`if`/`else`** statements.
day3.details: Review all major operators. Learn to chain conditions using **Logical Operators** (`&&`, `||`, `!`). Implement **`if`, `else if`, and `else`** clauses. Explore the concise **Ternary Operator** (`condition ? val1 : val2`) for simple conditional assignments. Introduce the **`switch`** statement for multi-case conditional logic.
day3.extra: Logical Operators (`&&`, `||`), Ternary Operator, `switch` statement, operator precedence, Falsy/Truthy values

day4.title: Loops & Iteration: Repetition
day4.definition: Mastering repetitive code execution using **`for`, `while`, `do...while`** loops and controlling loop flow.
day4.details: Use the traditional **`for` loop** for definite iteration and **`while` loop** for indefinite iteration (until a condition is false). Understand the use of **`break`** (exits the loop) and **`continue`** (skips the current iteration). Introduce **`for...of`** (for iterating over iterable values like arrays) and **`for...in`** (for iterating over object properties).
day4.extra: `for...of` vs `for...in`, `break` and `continue`, loop structure, definite vs indefinite iteration

day5.title: Functions: Reusable Code Blocks
day5.definition: Defining and using functions (**Declaration, Expression, Arrow**) to create reusable blocks of code and implementing parameters and return values.
day5.details: Cover the three main ways to define a function, highlighting **Arrow Functions (ES6+)** as the modern standard. Discuss **Default Parameters** and handling an unknown number of arguments using the **Rest Parameter (`...args`)**. Revisit **Function Scope** and the concept of **Hoisting** for function declarations vs. expressions.
day5.extra: Arrow Functions, Default Parameters, Rest Parameter (`...`), Hoisting (Function vs. Variable), `return` keyword

day6.title: Arrays: Core Methods & Manipulation
day6.definition: Working with ordered lists of data (**Arrays**) and utilizing fundamental array methods for adding, removing, and finding elements.
day6.details: Array creation and accessing elements by **index**. Essential methods: **`.push()`** (add to end), **`.pop()`** (remove from end), **`.shift()`** (remove from start), **`.unshift()`** (add to start), and **`.indexOf()`**. Learn non-mutating methods like **`.slice()`** (extracts a portion) versus mutating methods like **`.splice()`** (changes content).
day6.code:
const fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry'];

fruits.push('date'); // Mutates: adds to end
const first = fruits.shift(); // Mutates: removes first

const copy = fruits.slice(1, 3); // Non-mutating copy

console.log(fruits); // ['banana', 'cherry', 'date']
console.log(copy); // ['cherry', 'date']
day6.extra: Mutating vs. Non-mutating methods, Indexing, `.slice()`, `.splice()`, `.includes()`, Array Length

day7.title: Higher-Order Array Methods (Map, Filter, Reduce)
day7.definition: Harnessing the power of **Higher-Order Functions** to transform and process array data concisely using **`.map()`, `.filter()`, and `.reduce()`**.
day7.details: **`.map()`**: Transforms every element in an array and returns a new array of the same length. **`.filter()`**: Returns a new array containing only elements that pass a given test condition. **`.reduce()`**: Applies a function against an accumulator and each element to reduce the array to a single value. These methods are essential for modern, functional-style JavaScript and are generally preferred over traditional `for` loops for data processing.

day8.title: Objects: Key-Value Collections
day8.definition: Understanding and manipulating **Objects**, the core data structure for key-value collections in JavaScript (similar to Python's dictionaries).
day8.details: Objects store data in **unordered** key-value pairs. Access properties using **dot notation** (`obj.key`) or **bracket notation** (`obj['key']`). Learn methods for listing and iterating over properties: **`Object.keys()`**, **`Object.values()`**, and **`Object.entries()`**. Explore **Object Mutability** and how objects are passed by reference.
day8.extra: Dot vs. Bracket Notation, `Object.keys()`, `Object.values()`, Pass by Reference, Mutability of Objects

day9.title: ES6+ Features: Destructuring & Spread/Rest
day9.definition: Modernize code using ES6 features: **Destructuring** (Array/Object) for extracting data easily, and **Spread/Rest Operators** (`...`) for flexible data manipulation.
day9.details: **Object Destructuring** extracts properties into distinct variables. **Array Destructuring** does the same for array elements. The **Spread Operator (`...`)** is used to quickly copy or combine arrays and objects (creating a *shallow copy*). The **Rest Parameter (`...`)** collects multiple function arguments into a single array (already covered, but reviewed in the context of Destructuring).
day9.extra: Array and Object Destructuring, Spread Operator (`...`), Rest Parameter (`...`), Shallow Copy

day10.title: The 'This' Keyword & Prototypes
day10.definition: Deep dive into the **`this`** keyword, which changes context, and the concept of the **Prototype Chain** for inheritance.
day10.details: Understand that **`this`** refers to the *execution context*. Its value changes based on *how* a function is called (global, object method, constructor, or explicit binding). Explore the **Prototype Chain**; how objects inherit properties and methods from other objects (`__proto__`). Learn explicit binding using **`.call()`, `.apply()`, and `.bind()`** to manually set the `this` context. Arrow functions have a unique, lexically-scoped `this`.
day10.extra: `this` context, Prototype Chain, `call()`, `apply()`, `bind()`, Constructor functions, Lexical `this` in Arrow Functions

day11.title: Classes & OOP in JavaScript
day11.definition: Implementing **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** using **ES6 Classes** for object blueprints and using **Inheritance** to manage code structure.
day11.details: Classes in JavaScript are syntactic sugar over the prototype system. Define a **Class** with a **`constructor`** method (for initial state) and instance **Methods**. Implement **Inheritance** using the **`extends`** keyword, and use **`super()`** to call the parent class's constructor and methods. Differentiate between instance properties and static methods/properties.
day11.extra: Class Syntax, Constructor, `extends`, `super()`, Instance vs. Static methods, Encapsulation

day12.title: Modules: Import & Export
day12.definition: Organizing code into separate files (**Modules**) using the modern **`import` and `export` (ES Modules)** syntax for better reusability and dependency management.
day12.details: Understand the need for modules to prevent global scope pollution. Learn to use **Named Exports** (`export const name = ...`) and **Default Exports** (`export default ...`). Discuss the difference in importing them (`import { name } from './file.js'` vs. `import Name from './file.js'`). Briefly touch upon dynamic imports (`import('modulePath')`) for performance optimization.
day12.extra: Named vs. Default Exports, Module Bundlers (Webpack/Vite concept), Dynamic Imports, Global Scope Prevention

day13.title: Error Handling: Try/Catch/Finally
day13.definition: Making code robust by implementing **Error Handling** using **`try`, `catch`, `finally`** blocks, and knowing how to **`throw`** custom errors.
day13.details: Use the **`try`** block for code that may fail. The **`catch (error)`** block executes if an exception occurs, allowing for graceful recovery. The **`finally`** block executes regardless of whether an error occurred (useful for cleanup). Learn to create and throw custom `Error` objects (`throw new Error('Message')`) for specific application logic failures.
day13.extra: Specific Error Types (TypeError, ReferenceError), Throwing Errors, Stack Trace, `finally` block

day14.title: Asynchronous JS: Callbacks & Timers
day14.definition: Introduction to **Asynchronous JavaScript**, focusing on the **Event Loop** model and basic async patterns using **Callbacks** and built-in timer functions.
day14.details: Understand that JavaScript is single-threaded, but handles I/O operations non-blockingly using the **Event Loop**. Learn to use **`setTimeout()`** and **`setInterval()`** for delayed or repeated execution. Introduce the **Callback Pattern** (passing a function to be executed later), and the issues it creates (**'Callback Hell'**). This sets the stage for Day 15's solution: Promises.
day14.extra: Event Loop, Call Stack, Web APIs, Callbacks, `setTimeout`, `setInterval`, Callback Hell

day15.title: Asynchronous JS: Promises
day15.definition: Solving Callback Hell using **Promises**—objects that represent the eventual completion (or failure) of an asynchronous operation.
day15.details: A Promise has three states: **`pending`, `fulfilled` (resolved), and `rejected`**. Use **`.then()`** to handle a successful resolution and **`.catch()`** to handle errors/rejection. Learn **Promise Chaining** (`.then().then()`) for sequence-dependent asynchronous operations. Introduce utility methods like **`Promise.all()`** (wait for all to finish) and **`Promise.race()`** (finish on the first one).

day16.title: Asynchronous JS: Async/Await
day16.definition: Simplifying Promise handling by using the modern ES2017 syntax: **`async` and `await`**.
day16.details: The **`async`** keyword turns a function into one that returns a Promise. The **`await`** keyword pauses the execution of an `async` function until a Promise settles (resolves or rejects), making asynchronous code look and feel synchronous. Learn to wrap `await` calls in **`try/catch`** blocks for error handling, replacing the need for `.catch()`. This is the preferred pattern for modern asynchronous code.
day16.extra: `async` function, `await` keyword, Error handling with `try/catch`, Cleaner asynchronous code

day17.title: JSON & Fetch API: Networking
day17.definition: Understanding the **JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)** data format and using the built-in **`fetch` API** to make HTTP requests (GET, POST) to consume external APIs.
day17.details: Learn to convert between JSON strings and JavaScript Objects using **`JSON.stringify()`** and **`JSON.parse()`**. Use the browser's **`fetch()`** API (which returns a Promise) to retrieve data. Understand the process of handling the **Response object**, checking the **HTTP status code**, and calling `response.json()` to parse the response body.
day17.extra: `JSON.stringify()`, `JSON.parse()`, `fetch` API, HTTP Status Codes, Response object

day18.title: Closures & Higher-Order Functions
day18.definition: A deep dive into **Closures**: when an inner function remembers and can access variables from its parent scope even after the parent function has finished executing.
day18.details: Closures are fundamental to JS's power. Learn how they enable **Data Encapsulation**, **Private Variables**, and **Function Factories** (functions that return other functions). Explore **Higher-Order Functions (HOFs)** which either take a function as an argument (like `.map()`) or return a function (like a Closure factory).
day18.extra: Data Encapsulation, Private Variables, Function Factories, Currying (a HOF concept)

day19.title: Introduction to the DOM (Document Object Model)
day19.definition: Understanding the **DOM**—the programming interface for web documents—and learning how to select and inspect HTML elements.
day19.details: The DOM represents the HTML structure as a tree of objects. Learn essential selection methods: **`document.getElementById()`**, **`document.querySelector()`**, and **`document.querySelectorAll()`**. Understand the difference between NodeLists and HTMLCollections, and how to access and modify element attributes (like `id` or `class`).
day19.extra: DOM Tree structure, `getElementById`, `querySelector`, NodeList, HTMLCollection, Element Attributes

day20.title: DOM Manipulation: Content & Styling
day20.definition: Dynamically changing the content, structure, and appearance of elements on a webpage using JavaScript.
day20.details: Modify element content using **`.innerText`** (text only) and **`.innerHTML`** (HTML content). Change styling directly via the **`.style`** property (`element.style.color = 'red'`) and manage CSS classes using **`.classList`** (`.add()`, `.remove()`, `.toggle()`). Learn to create and insert new elements using **`document.createElement()`** and **`.appendChild()`**.

day21.title: DOM Events & Event Listeners
day21.definition: Responding to user interactions (like clicks, keypresses, form submissions) by registering **Event Listeners**.
day21.details: Understand the **Event Object** (which contains information about the event). Use **`.addEventListener()`** to attach functions to specific events (e.g., `'click'`, `'mouseover'`, `'submit'`). Learn about **Event Propagation** (Capturing and Bubbling) and using **`event.preventDefault()`** to stop default browser actions (e.g., stopping a form from submitting).

day22.title: Local Storage & Session Storage
day22.definition: Persisting simple data in the browser using the **Web Storage API** (Local and Session Storage).
day22.details: **Local Storage** stores data with no expiration date, surviving browser closure. **Session Storage** clears data when the browser tab is closed. Both store data as **key-value pairs** where values must be **strings**. Use **`localStorage.setItem()`**, **`localStorage.getItem()`**, and remember to use **`JSON.stringify()`/`JSON.parse()`** to store/retrieve complex objects.
day22.extra: `localStorage` vs `sessionStorage`, `setItem`, `getItem`, Data persistence, JSON for complex data

day23.title: Regular Expressions (Regex)
day23.definition: Using **Regular Expressions** to define text patterns for searching, validating, and manipulating strings.
day23.details: Learn basic Regex syntax: **Literals, Character Sets (`[]`), Quantifiers (`+`, `*`, `?`), and Anchors (`^`, `$`**). Use JavaScript's built-in methods that accept Regex: **`.test()`, `.exec()`, `string.match()`, `string.replace()`**. Focus on input validation (e.g., email or phone number formats).
day23.extra: Character Sets, Quantifiers, Anchors, Flags (g, i, m), `.test()`, `.match()`

day24.title: Set & Map: Unique Collections
day24.definition: Working with the **`Set`** (collection of unique values) and **`Map`** (ordered key-value pairs) objects (ES6+).
day24.details: A **`Set`** is ideal for quickly storing a list where duplicates are automatically removed, and for fast membership testing (`set.has(value)`). A **`Map`** is like an object but allows **any data type** (even objects or functions) to be used as keys, and maintains the **insertion order**. Learn the basic methods: **`.add()`, `.has()`, `.get()`, `.set()`**.
day24.extra: `Set` for uniqueness, `Map` for key flexibility, Insertion Order in Maps, `WeakMap`/`WeakSet` concept

day25.title: Data & Time Manipulation
day25.definition: Working with JavaScript's **`Date`** object for creating, formatting, and calculating time differences.
day25.details: Create `Date` instances (`new Date()`). Access date parts using methods like **`.getFullYear()`, `.getMonth()`, `.getDate()`, and `.getTime()`** (milliseconds since epoch). Focus on formatting dates for user display and using the powerful **`Intl.DateTimeFormat`** for internationalized date/time representation.
day25.extra: `.getTime()`, UTC vs Local Time, `Intl.DateTimeFormat`, Timezone handling

day26.title: DOM Mini-Project: Simple To-Do List
day26.definition: Combining skills from Days 19-21 to build a functional **To-Do List** application, focusing on state management and dynamic DOM rendering.
day26.details: Implement the core features: **1. Event Listener** for form submission, **2. Dynamic Element Creation** (`createElement`) for new list items, **3. Event Delegation** to handle clicks on dynamically created 'delete' buttons, and **4. Local Storage** integration (Day 22) to persist the list across page loads.
day26.extra: Project focus: Event Delegation, Dynamic Rendering, State management, Local Storage integration

day27.title: Functional Programming: Immutability & Purity
day27.definition: Introduction to **Functional Programming (FP)** principles: **Immutability** and **Pure Functions**.
day27.details: A **Pure Function** always returns the same result for the same input and causes no side effects (e.g., doesn't modify external data). **Immutability** means state is never changed; instead, a new state is created (using Spread or array methods). Understand why FP leads to more reliable, testable, and maintainable code. Review `.map()`, `.filter()`, and Spread (`...`) as immutable tools.
day27.extra: Pure Functions, Side Effects, Immutability, Referential Transparency

day28.title: Object Methods: Getters, Setters & Property Descriptors
day28.definition: Controlling how object properties are accessed and modified using **Getters and Setters** and understanding property configuration.
day28.details: **Getters** (`get propertyName()`) are functions that act like properties when accessed (computed values). **Setters** (`set propertyName(val)`) are functions that run when a property is assigned a value (used for validation). Learn to use **`Object.defineProperty()`** and **`Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptor()`** to control attributes like `writable`, `enumerable`, and `configurable`.

day29.title: Advanced Topics: Generators & Iterators
day29.definition: Working with the **Iterator Protocol** and creating custom, memory-efficient iterators using **Generator Functions** (`function*`) and the **`yield`** keyword.
day29.details: An object is **Iterable** if it defines a `Symbol.iterator` method. A **Generator** is a function that can be paused and resumed, producing a sequence of values on demand via **`yield`**. This is used for generating large sequences of data lazily and for asynchronous control flow (though mostly replaced by `async/await`).
day29.extra: Iterator Protocol, Generator Functions, `yield` keyword, Lazy Evaluation, `for...of` iteration

day30.title: Review & Final Project: Quiz App
day30.definition: Reviewing all core concepts and applying them to a substantial final project: a dynamic **Quiz Application**.
day30.details: This project integrates: **1. Data Structure** (Array of Question Objects), **2. DOM Manipulation** (loading questions, displaying answers), **3. Event Listeners** (handling user selections), **4. Control Flow** (checking answers, scoring), and **5. Functions** (to manage the application logic). The focus is on consolidating knowledge and moving beyond small snippets to a functional application.
day30.extra: Project focus: Full stack application logic, Review of Promises/Async, OOP concepts, Best practices

beginner.q: What is the difference between let, const, and var?
beginner.a: let: block-scoped, can be reassigned. const: block-scoped, cannot be reassigned. var: function-scoped, can be reassigned, hoisted.

beginner.q: How do you check the type of a variable?
beginner.a: Use typeof operator: typeof variable. Returns string like 'number', 'string', 'boolean', 'object', 'function', 'undefined'.

beginner.q: What is the difference between == and ===?
beginner.a: == checks value with type coercion, === checks value and type without coercion. Always use === for predictable results.

beginner.q: How do you write a function in JavaScript?
beginner.a: Function declaration: function name() {} or arrow function: const name = () => {} or function expression: const name = function() {}.

beginner.q: What is an array and how do you create one?
beginner.a: Arrays are ordered collections. Create with: const arr = [1,2,3] or const arr = new Array(1,2,3).

beginner.q: How do you add an element to an array?
beginner.a: push() adds to end, unshift() adds to beginning, or use spread: [...arr, newElement].

beginner.q: What is an object in JavaScript?
beginner.a: Objects are collections of key-value pairs. Create with: const obj = {key: 'value'} or const obj = new Object().

beginner.q: How do you access object properties?
beginner.a: Dot notation: obj.property or bracket notation: obj['property'] for dynamic keys or invalid identifiers.

beginner.q: What is a callback function?
beginner.a: A function passed as argument to another function, to be executed later (often after async operation completes).

beginner.q: What is JSON and how do you parse it?
beginner.a: JSON is data format. Parse with JSON.parse(string), stringify with JSON.stringify(object).

beginner.q: How do you handle errors in JavaScript?
beginner.a: Use try/catch blocks for synchronous code, .catch() for promises, or try/catch with async/await.

beginner.q: What is the DOM?
beginner.a: Document Object Model - programming interface for web documents, representing page as object tree.

beginner.q: How do you select an element by ID?
beginner.a: document.getElementById('id') or document.querySelector('#id').

beginner.q: What is event handling?
beginner.a: Responding to user interactions using addEventListener('event', handlerFunction).

beginner.q: What is a promise?
beginner.a: Promise represents eventual completion of async operation. Has states: pending, fulfilled, rejected.

beginner.q: How do you create a promise?
beginner.a: new Promise((resolve, reject) => { /* async operation */ }).

beginner.q: What is async/await?
beginner.a: Syntactic sugar for promises. async function returns promise, await pauses until promise settles.

beginner.q: What is hoisting?
beginner.a: JavaScript behavior where declarations are moved to top of scope. var and function declarations are hoisted.

beginner.q: What is closure?
beginner.a: Function that remembers and accesses variables from outer scope even when executed outside that scope.

beginner.q: What is the 'this' keyword?
beginner.a: Refers to object that is executing current function. Value depends on how function is called.

beginner.q: What are arrow functions?
beginner.a: Shorter syntax: (params) => expression. No own 'this', arguments, super, or new.target.

beginner.q: How do you loop through an array?
beginner.a: for loop, for...of, forEach(), map(), etc. for...of for values, for...in not recommended for arrays.

beginner.q: What is destructuring?
beginner.a: Extract values from arrays or properties from objects into distinct variables.

beginner.q: What is the spread operator?
beginner.a: ... expands iterables. Used for copying arrays/objects, merging, function arguments.

beginner.q: What is template literal?
beginner.a: Backtick strings allowing embedded expressions: `Hello ${name}` and multi-line strings.

beginner.q: What is NaN and how to check for it?
beginner.a: Not-a-Number. Check with isNaN(value) or Number.isNaN(value).

beginner.q: What is null and undefined?
beginner.a: undefined: variable declared but not assigned. null: intentional absence of value.

beginner.q: How do you convert string to number?
beginner.a: parseInt(string), parseFloat(string), Number(string), or unary plus: +string.

beginner.q: What is event bubbling?
beginner.a: Events propagate from target element up to document root. Use event.stopPropagation() to prevent.

beginner.q: What is localStorage?
beginner.a: Web storage API for persisting data in browser. localStorage.setItem(key, value), localStorage.getItem(key).

intermediate.q: Explain event delegation and its benefits.
intermediate.a: Attach single event listener to parent to handle events from children. Benefits: performance, dynamic elements, less memory.
JavaScript runtime model that handles async operations. Has call stack, callback queue, microtask queue.
setTimeout runs once after delay, setInterval runs repeatedly at intervals. Both return timer ID for clearing.
ES6 feature to split code into files. Export with export, import with import. Supports named and default exports.
Multiple .then() calls on promise. Each returns value for next .then(), or promise that resolves to value.

intermediate.q: What is the difference between let and var in loops?
intermediate.a: let creates new binding for each iteration, var shares same binding. Important in closures with setTimeout in loops.

intermediate.q: How does 'this' work in arrow functions?
intermediate.a: Arrow functions don't have own 'this'. They inherit 'this' from enclosing lexical scope.
Transforming function with multiple arguments into sequence of functions each with single argument.
Design pattern using IIFE and closures to create private and public members. Pre-ES6 module solution.
Functions that take other functions as arguments or return functions as results.

intermediate.q: Difference between deep copy and shallow copy?
intermediate.a: Shallow copy: nested objects shared. Deep copy: completely new object with all nested objects copied.

intermediate.q: How do you deep copy an object?
intermediate.a: JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(obj)) for JSON-safe objects, or use libraries like Lodash, or structuredClone() in modern JS.

intermediate.q: What is the purpose of use strict?
intermediate.a: Strict mode catches common errors, prevents unsafe actions, disables confusing features. Makes code more secure and optimized.
Mechanism where objects inherit properties from other objects via prototype linkage. Basis of JavaScript inheritance.

intermediate.q: What is the difference between class and prototype?
intermediate.a: class is syntactic sugar over prototype-based inheritance. Underneath, classes still use prototypes.

intermediate.q: How do you handle memory leaks?
intermediate.a: Remove event listeners, clear intervals/timeouts, avoid circular references, use weak references where appropriate.
Cross-Origin Resource Sharing - security feature that restricts cross-origin HTTP requests.
Allow running JavaScript in background threads, separate from main execution thread.

intermediate.q: Explain the Single Threaded Nature of JavaScript.
intermediate.a: JavaScript has one call stack, executes one thing at a time. Uses event loop for non-blocking I/O operations.
Functions that can be paused and resumed. Use function* and yield keywords.

intermediate.q: What is the difference between for...in and for...of?
intermediate.a: for...in iterates over enumerable properties (including inherited), for...of iterates over iterable values.
Primitive type for unique identifiers. Useful for object properties, avoiding name collisions.

intermediate.q: What is the purpose of Map and Set?
intermediate.a: Map: key-value collection with any key type. Set: collection of unique values. Both maintain insertion order.
Proxy: wrapper to intercept operations on objects. Reflect: built-in object with methods for interceptable operations.
?. operator allows safe access to nested properties without checking each level for null/undefined.
?? operator returns right-hand operand when left-hand is null or undefined (but not other falsy values).
Function that processes template literals. Receives string parts and interpolated values as arguments.

intermediate.q: What is the difference between attribute and property?
intermediate.a: Attributes are in HTML, properties are in DOM. Some sync, some don't. Properties represent current state.

intermediate.q: What is the purpose of data attributes?
intermediate.a: Custom attributes (data-*) to store extra information in HTML elements, accessible via dataset property.

advanced.q: Explain the JavaScript event loop in detail.
advanced.a: Runtime model with call stack, task queue (macrotasks), microtask queue. Microtasks (promises) run before macrotasks (setTimeout).

advanced.q: What is the difference between microtasks and macrotasks?
advanced.a: Microtasks: promise callbacks, queueMicrotask(). Macrotasks: setTimeout, setInterval, I/O. Microtasks run after current task, before next macrotask.

advanced.q: How does JavaScript handle memory management?
advanced.a: Automatic garbage collection. Mark-and-sweep algorithm. Memory leaks from global variables, closures, detached DOM nodes, timers.

advanced.q: What is the Temporal Dead Zone?
advanced.a: Period between entering scope and variable declaration where variable exists but cannot be accessed (for let/const).

advanced.q: Explain the module loading process in ES6.
advanced.a: Modules are parsed, imported modules fetched and parsed, then executed in depth-first order. Import hoisted to top.

advanced.q: What are Web Components?
advanced.a: Suite of technologies: Custom Elements, Shadow DOM, HTML Templates. Create reusable custom elements with encapsulated functionality.

advanced.q: How do you optimize JavaScript performance?
advanced.a: Debounce/throttle events, virtualize long lists, use Web Workers for heavy computation, code splitting, lazy loading.

advanced.q: What is Service Worker and its use cases?
advanced.a: Proxy between web app and network. Enables offline functionality, push notifications, background sync.

advanced.q: Explain the difference between Object.create and class inheritance.
advanced.a: Object.create creates object with specified prototype. Class uses constructor functions and prototype chain. Both achieve prototypal inheritance.

advanced.q: What is the purpose of Symbol.iterator?
advanced.a: Makes objects iterable. Defines default iterator for object, used by for...of and spread operator.

advanced.q: How do you implement a publish-subscribe pattern?
advanced.a: Create event emitter with subscribe (on), unsubscribe (off), and publish (emit) methods using object to store callbacks.

advanced.q: What is the difference between monkey patching and polyfilling?
advanced.a: Monkey patching: modifying existing objects at runtime. Polyfilling: adding modern features to older environments.

advanced.q: Explain the Revealing Module Pattern.
advanced.a: Variant of module pattern that explicitly defines public API at end, revealing only what's needed.

advanced.q: What are JavaScript decorators?
advanced.a: Proposed feature for modifying classes/methods at design time. Currently stage 3, requires transpiler.

advanced.q: How do you handle race conditions in async code?
advanced.a: Use flags, debouncing, cancellation tokens, or proper promise handling with Promise.race()/Promise.all().

advanced.q: What is the difference between static and dynamic import?
advanced.a: Static: analyzed at parse time, must be top-level. Dynamic: import() function returns promise, can be anywhere.

advanced.q: Explain the concept of immutability in JavaScript.
advanced.a: Data that cannot be changed after creation. Achieved with Object.freeze(), immutable libraries, or careful programming.

advanced.q: What are generator functions and when to use them?
advanced.a: function* with yield. Useful for lazy evaluation, async operations, infinite sequences, coroutines.

advanced.q: How do you implement memoization?
advanced.a: Cache function results based on arguments. Use closure to store cache, return cached result if arguments match.

advanced.q: What is the purpose of the Intl object?
advanced.a: ECMAScript Internationalization API for language-sensitive string comparison, number formatting, date and time formatting.

advanced.q: Explain the difference between passive and active event listeners.
advanced.a: Passive: cannot call preventDefault(), improves scroll performance. Active: can call preventDefault().

advanced.q: What is the difference between encoding and escaping?
advanced.a: Encoding: transforming data for transmission. Escaping: making data safe for specific context (HTML, URL, etc.).

advanced.q: How do you handle binary data in JavaScript?
advanced.a: Use ArrayBuffer, TypedArrays (Uint8Array, etc.), DataView, Blob, File APIs for binary data manipulation.

advanced.q: What is the purpose of the Performance API?
advanced.a: Provides timing information for performance monitoring: navigation timing, resource timing, user timing measurements.

advanced.q: Explain the difference between CSP and CORS.
advanced.a: CSP: Content Security Policy - whitelist allowed content sources. CORS: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing - control cross-origin requests.

advanced.q: What are the different JavaScript module formats?
advanced.a: IIFE, CommonJS (Node.js), AMD (RequireJS), UMD (universal), ES6 modules (standard).

advanced.q: How do you implement dependency injection in JavaScript?
advanced.a: Pass dependencies as parameters, use IoC containers, or framework-specific DI systems (Angular, etc.).

advanced.q: What is the difference between functional and object-oriented programming in JS?
advanced.a: FP: pure functions, immutability, composition. OOP: objects, classes, inheritance, encapsulation. JS supports both.

advanced.q: How do you handle security in JavaScript applications?
advanced.a: Input validation, output encoding, CSP headers, avoid eval(), use HTTPS, secure authentication, regular dependency updates.

coding.q: Write a function to reverse a string. What is its time complexity?
coding.a: One approach: return str.split('').reverse().join(''). Time complexity O(n) where n is string length.

coding.q: How to remove duplicates from an array?
coding.a: Use Set: [...new Set(array)] or filter with indexOf. Set approach is O(n), more efficient for large arrays.
Returns function that delays execution until after wait time has elapsed since last call. Useful for search inputs, resize events.

coding.q: Write a function to deep clone an object.
coding.a: Handle circular references, different data types. Can use recursion with proper type checking or structuredClone() in modern browsers.
Create class with then, catch, finally methods. Maintain state (pending, fulfilled, rejected) and callbacks queue.

coding.q: Write a function to flatten a nested array.
coding.a: Use recursion or iterative approach with stack. Handle different nesting levels efficiently.

coding.q: Implement a simple event emitter class.
coding.a: Include on (subscribe), off (unsubscribe), emit (publish) methods. Store events in object with array of callbacks.

coding.q: Write a function to find the most frequent element in array.
coding.a: Use object to count occurrences, track max count and corresponding element. O(n) time complexity.

coding.q: Implement a function to check if two objects are equal (deep comparison).
coding.a: Recursively compare properties, handle different types, circular references, and special cases like Date, RegExp.

coding.q: Write a function to group array elements by certain criteria.
coding.a: Use reduce to create object where keys are criteria and values are arrays of elements matching that criteria.
